content.text: Least-Privilege Secret Access Policies
Least privilege applied to secrets means every identity — CI runner, application pod, developer, admin — can read
exactly the secrets it needs
and nothing more. A CI runner that can read the production API key is a misconfigured CI runner. A developer who can write production secrets from their laptop is a breach waiting to happen.
Grant the minimum capabilities on the minimum paths to the minimum set of identities. If you can't articulate why a given identity needs a given secret path, it shouldn't have access.

This sub-subtopic covers how to design Vault policies for a Claude application with four identity types, how to audit existing access against the principle of least privilege, and how to enforce policy reviews as a recurring engineering task.

attrs.alt: Least-privilege access matrix: CI Runner, App Pod, Developer, and Admin mapped to secret paths and allowed capabilities

content.text: Four Vault Policies for a Claude Application
Each identity type gets its own Vault policy scoped to exactly the paths it needs:
The admin policy uses
sudo
capability for a small set of paths. Admin access requires MFA at login time and every access is logged — it should be used only during incidents or planned maintenance, never as a convenience.
Binding Policies to Identities
Policies alone do nothing — they must be bound to authentication roles so that specific identities receive specific policies when they authenticate:

# CI runner role: GitHub OIDC, scoped to main branch of specific repo
vault write auth/jwt/role/claude-ci \
  role_type="jwt" \
  bound_audiences="https://vault.example.com" \
  user_claim="sub" \
  bound_claims='{
    "repository": "org/claude-app",
    "ref": "refs/heads/main",
    "job_workflow_ref": "org/claude-app/.github/workflows/deploy.yml@refs/heads/main"
  }' \
  policies="claude-ci" \
  ttl="15m" \
  max_ttl="30m"

# App pod role: Kubernetes service account in prod namespace only
vault write auth/kubernetes/role/claude-prod-app \
  bound_service_account_names="claude-api" \
  bound_service_account_namespaces="prod" \
  policies="claude-prod-app" \
  ttl="1h"

# Developer role: authenticated via OIDC (Okta/Google), limited TTL
vault write auth/oidc/role/claude-dev \
  bound_audiences="vault" \
  allowed_redirect_uris="http://localhost:8250/oidc/callback" \
  user_claim="email" \
  bound_claims='{"groups": ["claude-engineers"]}' \
  policies="claude-dev" \
  ttl="8h"

# Admin role: OIDC + requires MFA
vault write auth/oidc/role/claude-admin \
  bound_audiences="vault" \
  allowed_redirect_uris="http://localhost:8250/oidc/callback" \
  user_claim="email" \
  bound_claims='{"groups": ["claude-admins"]}' \
  policies="claude-admin" \
  ttl="1h" \
  token_bound_cidrs="10.0.0.0/8"  # restrict to VPN range
Auditing Existing Access Against Least Privilege

Over time, policies accumulate capabilities that were added for convenience and never removed. An audit script compares the declared access matrix (a YAML truth file) against what Vault actually allows:

# access-matrix.yml — the intended access matrix (reviewed quarterly)
matrix:
  - identity: claude-ci
    paths:
      - path: "secret/data/ci/claude/*"
        capabilities: [read]
      - path: "database/creds/ci-test-role"
        capabilities: [read]

  - identity: claude-prod-app
    paths:
      - path: "secret/data/prod/claude/api_key"
        capabilities: [read]
      - path: "secret/data/prod/claude/webhook_secret"
        capabilities: [read]
      - path: "database/creds/prod-role"
        capabilities: [read]

  - identity: claude-dev
    paths:
      - path: "secret/data/dev/claude/*"
        capabilities: [read, list]
      - path: "secret/data/staging/claude/*"
        capabilities: [read]
Quarterly Access Review Process
Least privilege is a property that erodes over time as teams grow and access requirements change. A quarterly review enforces it structurally:
Review item
Owner
Action if stale
Access matrix YAML matches live Vault policies
Security lead
Update policy or matrix to resolve discrepancy
All Vault roles have appropriate bound_claims
Platform lead
Tighten or remove over-broad claims
Developer OIDC group membership is current
Engineering manager
Remove departed engineers from claude-engineers group
Admin role usage in audit log
Security lead
Flag any use outside incident/maintenance windows
Secret paths with no reads in 90 days
Platform lead
Candidates for deletion or archival

# List Vault audit log entries for admin role access in the last 30 days
# (Assumes audit log shipped to CloudWatch or similar)
vault audit list   # confirm audit is enabled

# Query the audit log file directly for admin token usage
grep '"display_name":"oidc-claude-admin"' /vault/logs/audit.log \
  | jq -r '[.time, .request.path, .request.operation] | @tsv' \
  | sort

# List all paths that have not been accessed recently
# (Vault Enterprise: vault operator usage-metrics)
# Open source: correlate audit log paths against declared matrix
awk -F'"' '/"path":/{print $4}' /vault/logs/audit.log \
  | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
Emergency Break-Glass Procedure
When the normal authentication path is unavailable (Vault unsealed but OIDC provider down, K8s API server unreachable), operators need a
break-glass
procedure that provides access while preserving auditability:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# break_glass.sh — emergency access to Vault with mandatory audit trail
# Requires: physical access to a break-glass envelope with root token fragment

set -euo pipefail
REASON="${1:?Usage: break_glass.sh '<reason for access>'}"

echo "BREAK-GLASS ACCESS INVOKED"
echo "Operator: $(whoami)@$(hostname)"
echo "Time: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
echo "Reason: ${REASON}"
echo ""
echo "This session is being logged to the audit trail."
echo "All access will be reviewed by the security team."
echo ""
read -r -s -p "Enter break-glass token: " VAULT_TOKEN
export VAULT_TOKEN

# Log the break-glass invocation
vault write sys/audit-hash/file/input \
  input="BREAK-GLASS: $(whoami) - ${REASON}"

# Confirm access is working
vault token lookup

echo ""
echo "Break-glass access granted. Shell session is audited."
echo "When done, revoke this token: vault token revoke -self"
Summary
Four policy tiers
: CI runner (ci secrets only), app pod (prod secrets only), developer (dev/staging read), admin (full + MFA + CIDR restriction)
Bound_claims
on Vault roles tie policy to specific OIDC attributes (repository, ref, group) — a compromised token from a different repo or branch cannot authenticate
Access matrix YAML
is the declared truth; audit script compares it against live Vault policies quarterly and blocks CI if discrepancies are found
Admin access
is audit-logged, CIDR-restricted, time-limited (1h TTL), and reviewed monthly — never used for routine operations
Quarterly review
covers policy-to-matrix alignment, role bound_claims, group membership, admin audit log, and unused secret paths
Break-glass procedure
provides emergency access with mandatory logging and post-incident review — the audit trail makes 'emergency' access auditable, not invisible
